segments.title: SU Comstock Construction Project Overview — Public Works Committee
segments.summary: Committee chair Dr. Chol Majok convenes Public Works committee to review Syracuse University's three-building sophomore housing initiative on Comstock Avenue. Hayner Hoyt Corporation president Jeremy Thurston and SU director Joel Fury present project scope: Waverly (762 beds) and Comstock (537 beds) residence halls, with Ostrom hall already underway. Foundation work began ~2 months ago; LaBella Associates is completing third-party building permit review. City administration explains the permit/fee structure and flags the core issue: an unprecedented 2-year full road closure on Comstock was not anticipated when the council adopted the new fee schedule last year. The initial 90-day administrative closure (approved by DPW commissioner) will expire end of March; any extension requires council approval.

segments.title: Road Closure Fee Schedule Debate — Precedent and Budget Deficit Tensions
segments.summary: Council scrutinizes the fee structure for Syracuse University's unprecedented 2-year Comstock Avenue road closure. Fees paid so far: $75 liability waiver and $1,500/meter for parking meter removal. The road cut permit — which triggers the compounding 14-day renewal fee schedule — has not been issued. Under current rules, fees could reach ~$2.6M for the full closure. Council raises concerns about precedent-setting: legislation should apply uniformly to all developers. A councilor invokes the city's $20M+ projected budget deficit, questioning whether fee modifications for development feasibility can be balanced against the city's own fiscal needs. Administration requests 90 more days (through end of June) to develop an approach for full road closures going forward.

political_signal.description: Council closely questions fee modification approach, raises precedent-setting concerns and invokes $20M budget deficit. Councilor: 'we're setting the precedent for what happens in the future' and 'we have a $20 million projected if not more deficit.'

processing_notes: $20 detected as dollar amount but refers to '$20 million projected deficit'
$75 and $1,500 are per the liability waiver fee schedule

segments.summary: Engineering staff explain the fee calculation: ~30,000 sq ft from curb to curb at unit pricing per the fee schedule, with 14-day renewal periods yielding the $2.6M two-year estimate. The scope could expand if closure extends property-line to property-line. CNS Engineers produced a vehicular/pedestrian traffic plan reviewed by city engineers: detours via Walnut and Marshall streets, fencing, flag personnel at entry/exit points. SU commits to full replacement of sidewalks, curbing, and road repaving upon project completion. A councilor pointedly notes 'Everything but our lost property taxes,' highlighting tension over SU's tax-exempt status and the city's investment in supporting university development.

political_signal.description: Chair requests detailed fee breakdowns per project. 'Everything but our lost property taxes' signals frustration with SU's tax-exempt status and the asymmetry of city costs vs. revenue from university construction.

processing_notes: $2 and $500 are fragments from fee schedule discussion ($2.6M total, $500/sq ft per unit pricing)
Speaker 'Miros' is from engineering department — surname garbled in auto-transcription

segments.title: Project Economics, Energy Design, and Pedestrian Safety Discussion
segments.summary: Discussion quantifies the full project economics: ~$600,000 in road closure fees per 90-day period, ~$1M building permit for two buildings, $253M hard construction cost for the Comstock dorms ($360M across all three SU residence halls), employing 600-700 workers. Buildings will use National Grid power with high-efficiency heat pumps but will not be energy-independent. Council clarifies that prior fatal dump truck incidents near SU campus were attributed to a separate University Ave housing project, underscoring pedestrian safety concerns. SPD recommended two new four-way stops on Ostrom Avenue, which have been well-received by the community. Chair thanks Hayner Hoyt and reaffirms the city's commitment to following its own permitting rules fairly.

political_signal.description: Chair signals council support for development balanced with fiscal accountability: 'We are making sure that we do the right thing in terms of the city permitting processes, making sure that we are following the rules that we have set in place which is fair for everybody.'

processing_notes: $360 refers to '$360 million in construction value' for all three residence halls
$500 is per the fee schedule unit pricing, not a standalone amount
Fatal dump truck incident referenced was from a separate SU-area project on University Ave
